title: Reading Descriptive Statistics
description: Use the Minnesota Sentencing Explorer to read means, medians, and distributions of presumptive sentence length, and learn what the gap between mean and median tells you about a distribution.
author: unmanaged
objectives: Describe what one row in the sentencing dataset represents
Apply an offense-code filter and read the resulting dataset summary
Distinguish the mean from the median of a numeric column
Infer distribution shape (skew) from the mean-vs-median gap

steps.title: What is in this dataset?
steps.body:
This tool explores roughly **294,000 Minnesota felony sentencing records** from **2001-2019**. Each row is a single sentenced count. Two columns you will use here:

- `sentyear` - the **Year Sentenced** (2001-2019).
- `time` - the **Presumptive Sentence** in **months** (what the sentencing guidelines recommend).

We will filter the data down to one kind of offense and then read the descriptive statistics the Explorer computes for `time`.

steps.title: Narrow to assault cases
steps.body:
The **MN Offense Code** is decoded one character at a time. Its first character, `moc1`, is the top-level offense category. `moc1 = A` means **Assault**.

We apply the filter `moc1 == A` and view the descriptive statistics for the presumptive-sentence column `time`. Note the reported **N** (how many rows survived the filter), the **mean**, and the **median**.

steps.title: Average presumptive sentence
steps.body: Reading the summary for `time` on the assault-filtered data (`moc1 == A`): to the nearest **0.1 months**, what is the **mean** presumptive sentence?

steps.title: Mean vs. median
steps.body: For these assault cases the **mean** of `time` is noticeably **larger** than the **median**. When the mean sits well above the median, the distribution is most likely...

options: Right-skewed (a long tail of long sentences)
Left-skewed (a long tail of short sentences)

steps.title: Does length vary by group?
steps.body: Descriptive statistics get more interesting when broken out by group. Here we cross-tabulate the mean of `time` by offender **`race`** (columns) and **`sex`** (rows), still within assault cases. Skim the table: do the cell means differ across groups?

steps.title: Confirm your filter
steps.body: Before finishing, confirm your working data is still filtered to assault offenses (`moc1 == A`). The next lesson builds on this filtered view.
